synthesis:
The most useful through-line this week: we’re getting better at **designing for two goals at once**—*make good decisions now* and *learn something you can trust later*. That’s true whether “later” means a tighter estimate of an effect size, a defensible choice of model complexity, or an AI agent you can certify with an actual coverage guarantee.

### Designing experiments when you care about action *and* inference

If you’ve ever run a sequential experiment in production, you know the tension: exploit the current best setting to reduce regret, but explore enough that you can *explain* what you learned (and quantify uncertainty). Xie et al. tackle that head-on in adaptive **combinatorial** designs (think selecting a bundle of factors/components each round), and they formalize the trade as a **Pareto frontier** between cumulative regret and “gap estimation error” (how well you can tell arm A from arm B).

What’s nice here is the practical knob: an exploration-decay parameter \(\alpha\) that visibly moves you along the frontier. Two algorithms match common feedback realities:

- **Full-bandit feedback** (you only see the aggregate response): MixCombKL uses KL/mirror-descent flavored updates.
- **Semi-bandit feedback** (you see per-item contributions): MixCombUCB uses UCB bounds plus randomized forced exploration.

The punchline for practitioners: **semi-bandit feedback buys you a meaningfully better frontier**, mostly because estimation error tightens a lot faster when you see item-level signals. If you’re designing data collection pipelines, this is another argument for instrumenting at the component level whenever you can.

### Borrowing strength from prior studies… without pretending you knew the prior

You et al. bring empirical Bayes into *optimal experimental design* in a way that feels tailor-made for messy applied work: you have related prior studies, often only as point estimates and standard errors, and you’d like your next experiment’s allocation to reflect that evidence.

They learn an EB prior (either parametric Gaussian or a nonparametric Gaussian-mixture NPMLE), then plug it into a Lindley-style design objective. The part worth leaning in on: they don’t just do “estimation optimality.” They derive EB-optimal **treatment propensities** under three distinct objectives:

- Minimize posterior estimation error (classic Bayes risk).
- Maximize in-experiment welfare (often produces “bang-bang” allocations).
- Maximize post-experiment policy value (an exploration–exploitation flavor via posterior dispersion).

And they prove an oracle-style result: as the number of prior studies grows, the EB design’s regret goes to zero, with explicit rates. Translation: if your org has a library of similar past experiments, you can justify “informative” allocations without hand-wavy priors.

### Reliability: choosing model size and certifying AI behavior

Martino et al. propose an **effective number of variables (ENV)** index that reads the whole error-vs-complexity curve \(V(k)\) geometrically (area under the curve, normalized), rather than hunting for an elbow that moves around depending on how far you extend \(k\). If you’ve ever watched an “elbow” slide as you add more candidate features, you’ll appreciate the motivation.

They also add decision-support measures—cumulative importance/uncertainty and a reliability-of-decision indicator—so you can say not only “pick 13 variables,” but also “and this choice is stable.”

Mouzouni et al., meanwhile, give us a surprisingly clean recipe for **black-box reliability certification of AI agents**: sample the agent multiple times per query (self-consistency), rank answers by frequency (with canonicalization to merge equivalent outputs), then apply **split conformal calibration** to output a prediction set of size \(M\) with finite-sample marginal coverage. Bias doesn’t “break” validity; it just forces larger sets. Their “reliability level” is essentially: how confident can we be while still letting the **mode alone** achieve conformal coverage?

For QE teams deploying LLM agents, this is actionable: you can turn stochastic sampling + conformal into a dashboardable readiness metric, and their sequential stopping rule cuts sampling cost roughly in half.

### Question to sit with

Across all four papers, the theme is *calibration under constraint*: regret vs estimation, welfare vs learning, simplicity vs overfitting, coverage vs cost. If we start treating these as explicit Pareto trade-offs (instead of hidden “defaults”), what would we change first—our instrumentation, our allocation rules, or our release gates?

papers.title: Adaptive Combinatorial Experimental Design: Pareto Optimality for Decision-Making and Inference
Using Prior Studies to Design Experiments: An Empirical Bayes Approach
An index of effective number of variables for uncertainty and reliability analysis in model selection problems
Black-Box Reliability Certification for AI Agents via Self-Consistency Sampling and Conformal Calibration
